article.title: TSMC's Arizona Fab Turns Profitable in Its First Full Year, Challenging Assumptions About Offshore Chip Manufacturing
article.summary: TSMC's first Arizona fab earned $514 million in 2025, its opening year of mass production, while Q1 2026 profit already exceeds that figure — upending the long-held view that advanced chip manufacturing outside Taiwan cannot be economically viable.
article.body_markdown:
## Overview

TSMC's first Arizona fabrication plant, Fab 21, earned $514 million in profit during its opening full year of mass production in 2025, according to [Tom's Hardware](https://www.tomshardware.com/tech-industry/semiconductors/tsmc-allocates-usd20-billion-to-arizona-expansion-project-faces-water-and-labor-shortages-complicated-by-visa-rules), citing Taiwan's National Development Council Minister Yeh Chun-Hsien. In the first three months of 2026 alone, the same facility surpassed that full-year figure, further solidifying what is described by the same report as "quite a big deal for foundries" — a first-year profit from a greenfield leading-edge chip factory built outside the country where such manufacturing has historically been concentrated.

The result directly challenges a thesis long associated with TSMC's founder: that building advanced chip plants in the United States is an expensive exercise unlikely to generate acceptable returns. The Arizona numbers suggest otherwise, and TSMC is acting on that confidence with a $20 billion capital injection into the Arizona subsidiary approved by its board.

## What We Know

### Fab 21 Financial Performance

Fab 21's first Arizona module entered mass production running TSMC's 4-nanometer-class process technology, according to [Tom's Hardware](https://www.tomshardware.com/tech-industry/semiconductors/tsmcs-arizona-fab-21-is-already-making-4nm-chips-yield-and-quality-reportedly-on-par-with-taiwan-fabs). Yields at the Phoenix facility are reportedly on par with TSMC's Taiwan fabs — a milestone Commerce Secretary Gina Raimondo characterized as "the first time ever in our country's history, we are making leading-edge 4nm chips on American soil, American workers — on par in yield and quality with Taiwan," per [Tom's Hardware](https://www.tomshardware.com/tech-industry/semiconductors/tsmcs-arizona-fab-21-is-already-making-4nm-chips-yield-and-quality-reportedly-on-par-with-taiwan-fabs).

Minister Yeh Chun-Hsien, who visited the Arizona campus, told Taiwanese officials that the startup phase "proceeded more smoothly than originally projected, which strengthens confidence in the long-term viability of the site," as reported by [Tom's Hardware](https://www.tomshardware.com/tech-industry/semiconductors/tsmc-allocates-usd20-billion-to-arizona-expansion-project-faces-water-and-labor-shortages-complicated-by-visa-rules). The $514 million figure was disclosed to him directly by TSMC.

### A Second Fab Ahead of Schedule

TSMC's second Arizona module — Fab 21 Phase 2 — has completed construction and will run 3-nanometer process technology, according to [Tom's Hardware](https://www.tomshardware.com/tech-industry/semiconductors/tsmc-brings-its-most-advanced-chipmaking-node-to-the-us-yet-to-begin-equipment-installation-for-3mn-months-ahead-of-schedule-arizona-fab-slated-for-production-in-2027). Equipment installation is now expected to begin in Q3 2026, with volume production targeted for the second half of 2027 — several quarters ahead of the original 2028 schedule, per the same [Tom's Hardware](https://www.tomshardware.com/tech-industry/semiconductors/tsmc-brings-its-most-advanced-chipmaking-node-to-the-us-yet-to-begin-equipment-installation-for-3mn-months-ahead-of-schedule-arizona-fab-slated-for-production-in-2027) report — making it the first time TSMC's most cutting-edge chipmaking node will have been installed on U.S. soil.

CEO C.C. Wei stated: "With the strong collaboration and support from our leading U.S. customers and the U.S. federal, state, and city government, we continue to speed up our capacity expansion in Arizona," as reported by [Tom's Hardware](https://www.tomshardware.com/tech-industry/semiconductors/tsmc-brings-its-most-advanced-chipmaking-node-to-the-us-yet-to-begin-equipment-installation-for-3mn-months-ahead-of-schedule-arizona-fab-slated-for-production-in-2027).

A third module — Fab 21 Phase 3 — targeting 2-nanometer and A16-class processes began construction in April 2025, according to [Tom's Hardware](https://www.tomshardware.com/tech-industry/semiconductors/tsmc-brings-its-most-advanced-chipmaking-node-to-the-us-yet-to-begin-equipment-installation-for-3mn-months-ahead-of-schedule-arizona-fab-slated-for-production-in-2027).

### Advanced Packaging and Capacity Expansion

At its 2026 North America Technology Symposium, TSMC announced that capacity for CoWoS (Chip on Wafer on Substrate) and SoIC advanced packaging is expected to grow at more than 80 percent annually through 2027, according to [Tribune India](https://www.tribuneindia.com/news/advanced-packaging/taiwan-tsmc-unveils-advanced-packaging-breakthroughs-and-2nm-roadmap-at-symposium). The company's 5.5-reticle-size CoWoS technology has already achieved yields exceeding 98 percent.

Looking further ahead, TSMC is developing a 14-reticle-size CoWoS platform scheduled for 2028 production, capable of integrating approximately 20 HBM (high-bandwidth memory) stacks into a single package, with an even larger version accommodating 24 HBM stacks targeted for 2029, per the same [Tribune India](https://www.tribuneindia.com/news/advanced-packaging/taiwan-tsmc-unveils-advanced-packaging-breakthroughs-and-2nm-roadmap-at-symposium) report.

On 2-nanometer process capacity, TSMC has already received approximately 25 finalized chip designs and has more than 70 additional customer projects in development, with 2nm capacity projected to grow at a 70 percent compound annual growth rate between 2026 and 2028, according to [Tribune India](https://www.tribuneindia.com/news/advanced-packaging/taiwan-tsmc-unveils-advanced-packaging-breakthroughs-and-2nm-roadmap-at-symposium).

### Process Roadmap Through 2029

Also at the symposium, TSMC extended its publicly disclosed process roadmap through 2029 with two new nodes. The A13 process is an optical shrink of A14, delivering around a 6 percent increase in transistor density, according to [TrendForce](https://www.trendforce.com/news/2026/04/23/news-tsmc-unveils-latest-roadmap-a12-a13-set-for-2029-without-high-na-euv-a16-volume-production-delayed-to-2027/). A12, also slated for 2029, adopts second-generation nanosheet gate-all-around transistors along with NanoFlex Pro technology for AI and HPC workloads. Notably, both nodes are designed to reach production without High-NA EUV lithography — a strategic divergence from Intel's 14A approach, which adopts High-NA EUV starting in 2027-2028.

A16, originally expected in volume production in 2026, has slipped to 2027, per [TrendForce](https://www.trendforce.com/news/2026/04/23/news-tsmc-unveils-latest-roadmap-a12-a13-set-for-2029-without-high-na-euv-a16-volume-production-delayed-to-2027/).

### Demand Context: Apple and Supply Strain

The profitable Arizona ramp coincides with a period of intensifying demand pressure on TSMC. Apple executives acknowledged that "limited chip availability for iPhone and Mac devices is currently weighing on growth," according to [TrendForce](https://www.trendforce.com/news/2026/05/05/news-apple-reportedly-eyes-samsung-intel-u-s-foundry-for-core-chips-amid-tsmc-constraints-supply-diversification/), as constrained 3nm capacity limits production of its latest A-series and M-series chips. Apple has held preliminary discussions with Intel and evaluated Samsung's Texas facility as part of a diversification effort, though no orders have resulted from either engagement and Apple continues to have "reservations about adopting non-TSMC manufacturing technologies," per the same source.

## What We Don't Know

The $514 million profit figure covers operating income at the Arizona subsidiary; it is not clear whether this calculation is fully loaded with the depreciation costs of the capital TSMC has deployed into Fab 21 Phase 1, or whether certain costs are amortized differently across the parent entity. At the consolidated group level, overseas fab ramp costs are known to weigh on total gross margins, meaning the overall company profitability still absorbs Arizona's startup costs even as the subsidiary itself turns profitable.

Water availability remains a structural uncertainty. TSMC has incorporated water recycling infrastructure, but the desert climate in Phoenix creates ongoing supply pressure, and [Tom's Hardware](https://www.tomshardware.com/tech-industry/semiconductors/tsmc-allocates-usd20-billion-to-arizona-expansion-project-faces-water-and-labor-shortages-complicated-by-visa-rules) notes that "water access remains one of the most pressing issues for the project due to the dry and hot climate in Arizona."

Labor availability is a second constraint. The Trump administration's $100,000 fees on new H-1B visa holders have complicated hiring of overseas technical staff, as reported by [Tom's Hardware](https://www.tomshardware.com/tech-industry/semiconductors/tsmc-allocates-usd20-billion-to-arizona-expansion-project-faces-water-and-labor-shortages-complicated-by-visa-rules), and TSMC is actively encouraging Taiwanese chemical and equipment suppliers to establish nearby U.S. operations to reduce logistical dependency on the island.

## Analysis

The Arizona profitability data, if sustained, has implications well beyond TSMC's own balance sheet. For years, the dominant industrial narrative held that advanced semiconductor fabrication was structurally anchored to Taiwan — that the density of suppliers, the depth of the local labor pool, and the scale effects of running multiple nodes on a single campus made replication elsewhere prohibitively costly. The Arizona result is the first concrete piece of production-scale evidence that this anchor can be loosened, even if not eliminated.

For policymakers in Washington who crafted the CHIPS Act precisely to test this thesis, the $514 million figure represents a validation milestone. For TSMC's competitors — Intel Foundry, which is itself racing to commercialize 18A in Arizona and Oregon, and Samsung Foundry, which operates its most advanced nodes in Taylor, Texas — the data raises the pressure on their own economics: if TSMC can turn a first-year profit in Phoenix on 4-nanometer chips, the argument that advanced overseas manufacturing is intrinsically loss-making becomes harder to sustain.
